title: The if Statement
description: Execute code only when a condition is True.

slides.title: The Basic if 🔑
slides.content:
# Your Gateway to Logic

The `if` statement is the most fundamental control structure:

```python
if condition:
    # This runs only if condition is True
    do_something()
```

It's simple but incredibly powerful!

slides.title: Simple if Examples 💻
slides.content: Let's see the if statement in various contexts:

quizData.question: If score = 75, which message prints?
quizData.explanation: 75 is not >= 90, so 'Excellent!' doesn't print. But 75 >= 70, so 'You passed!' prints!

slides.title: Multiple Statements 📝
slides.content:
# The Indented Block

All indented lines under `if` are part of the block:

```python
if logged_in:
    print("Welcome back!")
    show_dashboard()
    load_settings()
# This line runs regardless
print("End of program")
```

slides.title: Code Blocks 💻
slides.content: Multiple statements in an if block:
slides.codeSnippet:
is_premium = True

if is_premium:
    print("Thank you for subscribing!")
    print("Enjoy ad-free experience")
    print("Access all features")

print("Welcome to our app!")

quizData.question: How does Python know what's inside an if block?
quizData.explanation: Python uses indentation to define code blocks — everything indented at the same level is part of the block!

slides.title: Compound Conditions 🔗
Compound Conditions 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
age = 25
has_license = True
has_insurance = True

if age >= 18 and has_license and has_insurance:
    print("You can rent a car!")

is_weekend = True
is_holiday = False

if is_weekend or is_holiday:
    print("Time to relax!")

quizData.question: if True and False: runs the block?
quizData.explanation: With 'and', both must be True. True and False = False, so the block doesn't run.

slides.title: Membership Tests 📋
Membership Checks 💻

quizData.question: What does 'in' check for?

options.text: If a value exists in a collection
If a number is inside a range

quizData.explanation: The 'in' operator checks if a value exists in a sequence like a list, string, or tuple!

slides.title: Nested Parentheses 📐
slides.content:
# Grouping Complex Conditions

Use parentheses for clarity:

```python
if (age >= 18 and has_id) or is_vip:
    allow_entry()
```

Without parentheses, precedence rules apply (`and` before `or`).

slides.title: Grouped Conditions 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
score = 75
attempts = 2
bonus = True

# Without parentheses (confusing)
# if score > 80 or score > 70 and bonus:

# With parentheses (clear!)
if (score > 80) or (score > 70 and bonus):
    print("You qualify!")

# Even clearer
has_high_score = score > 80
has_bonus_pass = score > 70 and bonus
if has_high_score or has_bonus_pass:
    print("Qualification confirmed!")

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Early Returns

In functions, use early returns to reduce nesting:

```python
def process_user(user):
    if not user:
        return  # Exit early
    
    if not user.is_active:
        return  # Exit early
    
    # Main logic here
    process(user)
```

quizData.question: What does 'if x:' check?

options.text: If x is truthy (not 0, empty, None)
If x exists as a variable
If x is a valid type

quizData.explanation: 'if x:' checks if x is truthy — any non-zero, non-empty, non-None value!
